one_liner: A 29-year-old man presents with 2 weeks of fatigue, anorexia, nausea, right upper quadrant discomfort, and dark urine. He recently noticed yellowing of his sclera and developed a serum sickness–like rash and joint pains.

stage2.hpi_extension: He reports a 2-week prodrome of malaise, fatigue, nausea, low-grade fever, and RUQ discomfort, followed by dark (tea-colored) urine, acholic stools, and scleral icterus. He also has migratory arthralgias and a transient urticarial rash ('serum sickness–like syndrome'). He had unprotected sex with new partners 3 months ago and occasionally shares tattoo equipment. He denies IV drug use but has had multiple sexual partners.
stage2.social_history: Bartender. Smokes occasionally. Drinks 3–4 beers on weekends. Multiple recent sexual partners. Unvaccinated against hepatitis B.
stage2.physical_exam: Scleral icterus and jaundice. Skin: faint urticarial wheals on the trunk. Abdomen: mild tender hepatomegaly in the right upper quadrant; no splenomegaly, ascites, or stigmata of chronic liver disease. Neuro: alert, no asterixis. Joints: mild tenderness of bilateral wrists and MCPs without effusion.

lab_results.result: AST 1,850 U/L and ALT 2,240 U/L (markedly HIGH, ALT > AST). Total bilirubin 8.4 mg/dL (HIGH, direct predominant). Alk phos mildly elevated. INR 1.3. Albumin 3.8 g/dL.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Dramatic transaminase elevation (>1,000) with ALT > AST and hyperbilirubinemia is classic for acute viral hepatitis. INR and albumin assess synthetic liver function.

lab_results.result: HBsAg POSITIVE. HBeAg POSITIVE (high infectivity). IgM anti-HBc POSITIVE. Anti-HBs negative. Anti-HBe negative. HBV DNA by PCR: >100,000,000 IU/mL. Anti-HCV negative. IgM anti-HAV negative. HIV negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: HBsAg + IgM anti-HBc positive defines ACUTE hepatitis B. HBeAg reflects active viral replication and high infectivity. IgM anti-HBc is the only serologic marker during the 'window period' when HBsAg has cleared and anti-HBs has not yet appeared.

lab_results.result: Liver biopsy (not typically needed acutely): hepatocellular swelling (ballooning degeneration), spotty necrosis, lymphocytic infiltration, and characteristic 'ground-glass' hepatocytes (HBsAg-filled smooth endoplasmic reticulum) on later specimens if disease becomes chronic.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Ground-glass hepatocytes are pathognomonic for hepatitis B infection and reflect accumulation of HBsAg within the hepatocyte ER.

stage5.final_clue: He is managed supportively as an outpatient. Over 8 weeks his transaminases normalize, HBsAg clears, and he develops anti-HBs and anti-HBc IgG — indicating resolved acute hepatitis B with immunity. His sexual partners are offered HBIG + hepatitis B vaccination for post-exposure prophylaxis.

debrief.classic_presentation: Acute hepatitis B classically presents after a 1–6 month incubation with a prodrome of constitutional symptoms (fatigue, anorexia, nausea, low-grade fever, RUQ pain), followed by an icteric phase with jaundice, dark urine, acholic stools, and tender hepatomegaly. A serum sickness–like syndrome (fever, urticaria, migratory arthralgias) may precede symptoms due to immune complex deposition. Most adults (>95%) clear acute infection; ~5% progress to chronic infection. Neonatal vertical transmission is the opposite — ~90% become chronic carriers.
debrief.pathophysiology: Hepatitis B virus is a partially double-stranded, enveloped DNA virus (Hepadnavirus) that uses reverse transcriptase to replicate. Transmission is parenteral, sexual, and perinatal. HBV is not directly cytopathic; rather, cytotoxic T lymphocytes targeting HBV antigens on hepatocytes cause liver injury. Serologic markers: HBsAg = current infection (acute or chronic); anti-HBs = immunity (vaccination or resolution); HBeAg = active replication/high infectivity; IgM anti-HBc = acute infection (only marker in 'window period'); IgG anti-HBc = past exposure. Chronic infection predisposes to cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma (HBV integrates into host DNA and can cause HCC even WITHOUT cirrhosis).
debrief.treatment_overview: Acute hepatitis B in adults is usually self-limited and treated supportively. Chronic hepatitis B is treated with nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (entecavir, tenofovir) or pegylated interferon-alpha. Post-exposure prophylaxis: HBIG + hepatitis B vaccine. Universal vaccination (recombinant HBsAg) is the mainstay of prevention. Pregnant HBsAg-positive women should receive antiviral therapy in the third trimester and their infants receive HBIG + vaccine at birth to prevent vertical transmission.
high_yield_pearls: HBV is the ONLY DNA hepatitis virus — partially double-stranded, enveloped Hepadnavirus; replicates via reverse transcriptase.
Serology: HBsAg + IgM anti-HBc = acute. Anti-HBs only = vaccinated. Anti-HBs + anti-HBc IgG = resolved/immune. HBsAg >6 months = chronic.
Window period: HBsAg has cleared but anti-HBs not yet detectable — diagnose with IgM anti-HBc.
HBeAg = 'e' for envelope; indicates high infectivity and active replication. HBV DNA PCR quantifies viral load.
Extrahepatic manifestations: polyarteritis nodosa (PAN), membranous nephropathy, serum sickness–like syndrome. Chronic HBV can cause HCC even WITHOUT cirrhosis (integrates into host DNA).
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Gastrointestinal chapter, 'Hepatitis viruses — Hepatitis B virus' section.
